description: This figure shows schematic diagrams of mechanical test specimens for Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel with varied nitrogen contents. Subplot (a) presents the dimensional design of the tensile test specimen: total length 114 mm, grip section diameter Ø16 mm (length 20 mm per side), gauge section diameter Ø10 mm (length 50 mm), transition fillet radius R10. Subplot (b) illustrates the Charpy impact test specimen: total length 50 mm, cross-section size 10 mm × 10 mm, 45° V-shaped notch with 8 mm residual thickness below the notch. These machined specimens are applied for room-temperature tensile tests (strain rate 1×10⁻³ s⁻¹, tensile speed 3 mm/min) and impact toughness tests, to characterize mechanical property differences induced by nitrogen addition, supporting the research on nitrogen effects on microstructure and mechanical performance of the target low-density steel system.

This figure presents room-temperature tensile stress-strain curves of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels, with strain (in %) as the horizontal axis and stress (in MPa) as the vertical axis. Six tested samples include three low-nitrogen (LN) parallel samples (LN1, LN2, LN3) and three high-nitrogen (HN) parallel samples (HN1, HN2, HN3). All samples show a similar linear elastic stage, with yield strength ranging from 647 MPa to 685 MPa. The LN group has an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of ~832.5 MPa and fracture strain of 58% to 68%, while the HN group has a slightly higher UTS of ~841.5 MPa and significantly larger fracture strain of 68% to 78%. HN steels exhibit better plasticity, later fracture, and higher fracture energy consumption than LN steels. These results support the core conclusion that nitrogen addition improves the UTS, plasticity and toughness of the studied low-density steel while slightly reducing its yield strength, consistent with the grain refinement effect induced by fine AlN inclusions in HN steels.

This figure displays the microstructures of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels, with subfigure (a) for low-nitrogen (LN) steel and subfigure (b) for high-nitrogen (HN) steel, both equipped with a 50 μm scale bar. The dual-phase microstructure comprises an austenite matrix and ferrite precipitated at austenite grain boundaries. LN steel possesses coarser stripe-like ferrite with a higher area fraction of 5.08%, and its average austenite grain size is 20.5 μm. In comparison, HN steel has much finer, lower-content ferrite (area fraction 1.84%) and significantly refined austenite grains with an average size of 12.1 μm. This comparison verifies that nitrogen addition stabilizes austenite, inhibits ferrite formation, and promotes grain refinement, supporting the conclusion that nitrogen addition enhances tensile strength and plasticity but reduces yield strength due to the decreased ferrite content.

This figure presents two scanning electron microscopy micrographs showing typical morphologies of AlN inclusions in high-nitrogen (HN) Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel, with each subplot marked with a 2 μm scale bar. Subplot (a) displays an intact, single polyhedral AlN inclusion with straight edges and sharp angles, representative of as-precipitated AlN in the HN steel matrix. Subplot (b) exhibits a fractured, aggregated AlN inclusion that cracked during the multi-directional hot forging process the steel underwent. AlN is confirmed as the dominant inclusion type in HN steel, where 84% of AlN inclusions are smaller than 2 μm and contribute to austenite grain refinement, while broken aggregated AlN inclusions act as preferred crack initiation sites during material fracture, negatively affecting mechanical properties. This morphology observation supports the correlation between AlN inclusion characteristics, processing history, and mechanical performance of nitrogen-alloyed low-density steels.

This set of scanning electron microscopy images shows typical inclusion morphologies in low nitrogen (LN) Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel. Subfigure (a) exhibits an elliptical Mn(Mg)S sulfide inclusion with smooth edges, marked with a 2 μm scale bar. Subfigure (b) displays a polyhedral structured AlN inclusion, marked with a 1 μm scale bar. Subfigure (c) shows a complex AlN-Mn(Mg)S inclusion with Mn(Mg)S precipitated on the surface of the inner AlN core, marked with a 2 μm scale bar. These observations verify that the main inclusions in LN steel are Mn(Mg)S, AlN, and their composite inclusions, providing direct morphological evidence for analyzing the correlation between nitrogen content, inclusion characteristics, microstructure and mechanical properties of the studied low-density steel system.

This stacked bar chart presents the number percentage distribution of inclusions grouped by four size categories (<2 μm, 2-4 μm, 4-6 μm, >6 μm) in two Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel samples: low-nitrogen (LN) steel and high-nitrogen (HN) steel. For LN steel, inclusions are distributed as 39% <2 μm, 39% 2-4 μm, 12% 4-6 μm, and 10% >6 μm, with 78% of inclusions smaller than 4 μm. For HN steel, 84% of inclusions (dominantly AlN) are <2 μm, 11% 2-4 μm, 4% 4-6 μm, with no inclusions larger than 6 μm, so 95% of inclusions are smaller than 4 μm. The comparison demonstrates that nitrogen addition drastically increases the proportion of fine inclusions under 2 μm and reduces average inclusion size. This evidence supports that fine dispersed AlN inclusions act as heterogeneous nucleation sites to refine austenite grains, contributing to enhanced mechanical properties of HN steel.

This bar chart presents the number density of inclusions (unit: # mm⁻²) as a function of inclusion size ranges (<1 μm, 1–2 μm, 2–3 μm, up to 7–8 μm) for two Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel variants: low-nitrogen (LN, peach hatched bars) and high-nitrogen (HN, green hatched bars). HN steel exhibits a far higher total inclusion number density, with the peak value (~72 # mm⁻²) occurring in the 1–2 μm size range, followed by ~40 # mm⁻² for <1 μm inclusions. In comparison, LN steel has a lower overall inclusion density, with its peak density (~21 # mm⁻²) also in the 1–2 μm range, and higher density than HN for inclusions larger than 3 μm. This data verifies that increased nitrogen addition promotes formation of a large number of fine (<2 μm) AlN inclusions, which supports the conclusion that these fine inclusions act as heterogeneous nucleation sites to refine austenite grains in high-nitrogen steel.

This figure presents the AlN stability phase diagram of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel, with x-axis representing [Al] content in wt% and y-axis representing [N] content in wt%. Three equilibrium curves are included: the solid line corresponds to smelting temperature (1873 K), the blue dotted line to liquidus temperature (1587 K), and the red dash-dot line to solidus temperature (1493 K). The area above these curves is the AlN saturation region, while the area below is the region where AlN cannot stably exist. Composition points of two experimental steels are marked: LN steel (9.90 wt% Al, 0.0025 wt% N) is located below the 1873 K equilibrium line, verifying AlN is not a stable phase and cannot form during smelting; HN steel (9.95 wt% Al, 0.1326 wt% N) lies far above the equilibrium line, proving AlN inclusions can easily form and remain stable during the smelting process. This diagram confirms the difference in AlN inclusion formation tendency between low-nitrogen and high-nitrogen tested steels.

This figure presents a calculated pseudobinary phase diagram of the Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C–N system, with x-axis showing mass fraction of nitrogen ranging from 0.0 to 2.0 wt% and y-axis showing temperature ranging from ~700 K to 1800 K. Multiple phase regions are clearly labeled, covering equilibrium phase combinations of liquid phase (L), δ-ferrite, γ-austenite, α-ferrite, AlN, M₅C₂ and M₂₃C₆ precipitates. Calculated via Thermo-Calc software with the TCFE8 database, the diagram reveals that with increasing nitrogen content, the maximum formation temperature and stability temperature range of austenite significantly increase, while ferrite phase gradually disappears. As an austenite stabilizing element, nitrogen expands the austenite phase region and suppresses ferrite growth, which provides thermodynamic support for the experimentally observed reduction in ferrite content and austenite grain refinement in nitrogen-alloyed low-density steel.

This figure presents tensile fracture morphologies of low-nitrogen (LN, subfigure a) and high-nitrogen (HN, subfigure b) Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steels. Each subfigure consists of three parts: a 3D reconstructed macroscopic fracture profile with height color scale (maximum 12.19 mm for LN, 8.09 mm for HN), low-magnification SEM image of the full fracture surface (1 mm scale bar), and high-magnification SEM micrograph of microscale fracture features (5 μm scale bar). LN steel exhibits a ~45° shear fracture surface, with smaller, higher-density dimples indicating more severe local stress concentration during deformation, earlier necking and lower fracture energy. HN steel shows significantly larger macroscopic necking, a crater-shaped fracture, and larger, deeper, more uniformly distributed dimples, which demonstrates its better plasticity and toughness. These fracture features confirm that nitrogen addition improves the tensile plasticity and fracture resistance of the studied low-density steel, consistent with its higher measured elongation and ultimate tensile strength.

This figure presents impact fracture morphologies of two Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel samples: low nitrogen (LN) steel in subfigure (a) and high nitrogen (HN) steel in subfigure (b). Each subfigure includes three components: a 3D height topography map of the fracture surface (with height color scales ranging 0–7.53 mm for LN, 0–9.95 mm for HN), a low-magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the full fracture cross-section (1 mm scale bar, with a marked region for high-magnification observation), and a high-magnification SEM image of fracture dimples (10 μm scale bar). Both samples exhibit typical ductile fracture features, containing fibrous, radiation, and shear lip zones. LN steel shows elongated dimples oriented along the crack propagation direction, indicating shear force-accelerated crack growth. HN steel features larger, deeper, more uniform dimples and a significantly higher shear lip area ratio than LN steel, demonstrating slower crack growth rate, higher deformation energy absorption, and better impact toughness, supporting that nitrogen addition enhances the toughness of this low-density steel system.

This set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images shows typical inclusions located in dimples on the fracture surface of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel. Subfigure (a), with a 10 μm scale bar, identifies large deformable Mn(Mg)S sulfide inclusions in dimples, which act as fracture initiation points and feature weak interfacial bonding with the steel matrix. Subfigure (b), with a 2 μm scale bar, presents aggregated brittle AlN inclusions in dimples of high-nitrogen (HN) grade of the steel: fine dispersed AlN inclusions (84% smaller than 2 μm) promote austenite grain refinement and improve mechanical properties, while aggregated AlN formed during smelting tends to trigger crack initiation and degrade material performance. This figure validates that nitrogen addition modulates inclusion type and distribution, which is a key factor governing the fracture behavior and mechanical properties of the studied low-density steel.
